title: News from Science Weekly Headlines
html_body:

Now accepting entries for the US$ 25,000
Eppendorf & Science Prize for Neurobiology
Application Deadline June 15, 2026
www.eppendorf.com/prize
This week’s headlines
Help News from Science deliver the deep, trustworthy reporting that this challenging time requires.
Support nonprofit science journalism—make a tax-deductible gift.
Latest News
Hormone linked to morning sickness may help reduce alcohol intake
GDF15, which is thought to contribute to nausea during pregnancy, may keep our drinking in check, researchers propose
By CATHERINE OFFORD  |  20 MAR
Read more
Latest News
Microscopic spikes on snakeskin block bacterial buildup
Structures on python scales could inspire chemical-free antimicrobial designs
By ADA MCVEAN  |  20 MAR
Read more
ScienceInsider
First science from private Moon lander challenges lunar divide
Blue Ghost data suggest NASA’s growing commercial Moon program can deliver results
By PAUL VOOSEN  |  20 MAR
Read more
ScienceInsider
Energy Department merges nuclear and particle physics programs, unsettling researchers
In spite of synergies, physicists worry both fields are being sidelined in favor of AI
By ADRIAN CHO  |  20 MAR
Read more
Latest News
How realistic is the science in Project Hail Mary?
From the viability of waterless life to how researchers should handle cosmic emergencies, astronomer Wendy Freedman weighs in on the new sci-fi film
By PERRI THALER  |  19 MAR
Read more
Latest News
Debate explodes over age of key South American archaeological site
New study argues Monte Verde is far younger than once thought, challenging when people arrived in the Americas
By LIZZIE WADE  |  19 MAR
Read more
Latest News
Rice needs to be replanted every year. Genetic tinkering could make it more like apples
Researchers re-create key aspects of long-lived wild relative, which could make rice fields longer lasting
By ERIK STOKSTAD  |  19 MAR
Read more
News Feature
What happened when an Arab neuroscientist took the helm at an Israeli university?
Mouna Maroun’s stewardship highlights the promise—and perils—of reconciliation through science
19 MAR
Read more
Latest News
Floating wetlands boost water quality, slash greenhouse emissions
Fabricated platforms at an Australian wastewater lagoon soak up water pollution and methane
By CLAUDIA STEINER  |  19 MAR
Read more
ScienceInsider
ArXiv, the pioneering preprint server, declares independence from Cornell
As an independent nonprofit, it hopes to raise funds to cope with exploding submissions and “AI slop”
By JEFFREY BRAINARD  |  18 MAR
Read more
Latest News
China demands evidence that traditional medicine injections really work
New regulations ask manufacturers to provide efficacy and safety data—or withdraw their products
By DENNIS NORMILE  |  18 MAR
Read more
Latest News
Mysterious type of static electricity has a hidden culprit
Find explains how volcanic ash plumes spark lightning and why dust in grain silos can explode
By RACHEL BERKOWITZ  |  18 MAR
Read more
Latest News
Mitochondria packaged in blood cell membranes improve disease symptoms in mice
Tiny capsules can deliver healthy organelles into animals with Parkinson’s-like and other mitochondria-linked disorders
By CATHERINE OFFORD  |  18 MAR
Read more
ScienceInsider
Computer science’s ‘Nobel Prize’ goes quantum
Charles Bennett and Gilles Brassard share this year’s $1 million A.M. Turing Award for work on quantum information
By ADRIAN CHO  |  18 MAR
Read more
Latest News
Scientific conferences can be a bore. Can jokes liven them up?
Science speaks with ecologist Stefano Mammola about the power of humor to enhance science communication
By PHIE JACOBS  |  17 MAR
Read more
ScienceInsider
White House lifts hold on NIH research spending
Director Jay Bhattacharya tells House lawmakers that grantmaking, hiring are on track
By JOCELYN KAISER  |  17 MAR
Read more
Latest News
Colliding currents can target the deep brain without surgery
Human studies show promise of stimulation approach for epilepsy and other diseases
By JENNIE ERIN SMITH  |  17 MAR
Read more
ScienceShots
This hot pink cricket is no mutant
Arota festae’s unusual hue may help it blend in with immature plants
By TAYLOR MITCHELL BROWN  |  17 MAR
Read more
ScienceInsider
Research on fruit flies and other ‘model’ organisms may be declining
Analysis of published papers on eight widely studied species suggests work on them is fading, but not everyone is worried
By MITCH LESLIE  |  17 MAR
Read more
Latest News
Ancient DNA reshapes the story of early Pacific migrations
Neolithic seafarers who settled Palau were already a blend of Asian and Papuan peoples
By CHRISTA LESTÉ-LASSERRE  |  16 MAR
Read more
Latest News
Can potatoes grow on the Moon?
Experiments show the crop can survive in lunar soil—with a lot of help
By PERRI THALER  |  16 MAR
Read more
ScienceInsider
Analysis: Why the research money isn’t flowing from NSF and NIH
White House review of agency spending plans for this year is causing delays
By JEFFREY MERVIS  |  16 MAR
Read more
ScienceInsider
India’s national AI platform tackles the country’s many tongues
Bhashini aims to put the power of AI in the hands of all Indians
By T.V. PADMA  |  16 MAR
Read more
Latest News
A Brazilian scientist’s experimental paralysis therapy is fueling hope—and hype
Spinal cord injury patients are scrambling for access to the injection, but researchers say preliminary evidence is weak
By LUIZ FELIPE FERNANDES  |  16 MAR
Read more
ScienceInsider
A forgotten social media post may hold key clues to COVID-19’s origin
Analysis of Wuhan market map suggests China has not disclosed some of the earliest infections in animals and people
By JON COHEN  |  16 MAR
Read more
Latest News
Bronze Age loom weaves a tale of ancient textiles
A collapsed roof preserved wood from one of the world’s oldest weaving machines
By ANDREW CURRY  |  15 MAR
Read more
ScienceInsider
India scraps new earthquake hazard map and building codes, drawing pushback from geologists
Concerns about cost and practicality sparked backlash from developers and engineers
By VAISHNAVI CHANDRASHEKHAR  |  13 MAR
Read more
Latest News
Earth’s first major extinction was worse than we thought
Fossil finds suggest nearly 80% of life on Earth died some 550 million years ago
By TAYLOR MITCHELL BROWN  |  13 MAR
Read more
ScienceInsider
United States is cutting ties with influential global cancer agency
Trump’s pullout from WHO bars federal scientists from working with the International Agency for Research on Cancer and could slash its funding
By JOCELYN KAISER  |  13 MAR
Read more
ScienceInsider
NASA kills future x-ray mission
$1 billion AXIS proposal fell victim to agency upheaval, principal investigator says
By DANIEL CLERY  |  13 MAR
Read more
